original_hypothesis.title: coreCallback Sweeps Entire ERC20 Balance Allowing Theft of Stuck Tokens
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In `MarginTradingHook.coreCallback`, the contract transfers its entire balance of `tokenIn` to the swap helper (`uint amtIn = IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).balanceOf(address(this))`). An attacker can steal any stuck ERC20 tokens in the hook by initiating a margin position that uses the stuck token as `tokenIn`.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Using `balanceOf(this)` instead of a tracked amount allows the next user of that token to absorb all residual balances into their swap, effectively stealing them.
preconditions: ERC20 tokens are accidentally sent to or left in the `MarginTradingHook`.

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Uses full contract balance for the swap.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls `increasePos` with `tokenIn` matching the stuck token.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: The stuck tokens are added to the attacker's swap, resulting in the attacker receiving more collateral than they paid for.

economic_questions: Can this be combined with a donation attack to manipulate other users' slippage?
validation_requirements: Donate USDC to the hook, then call `increasePos` with 1 USDC and verify the collateral minted reflects the donated amount.

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal capital to open a position.

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When tokens are observed stuck in the contract.

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who accidentally transfer tokens to the hook.
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ERC20 tokens in the hook.

validation_result.impact_summary: Any ERC20 tokens stuck in MarginTradingHook can be swept by the next user who initiates a margin position using that token as the borrow token, giving them unearned collateral.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `coreCallback` function in `MarginTradingHook.sol` uses `IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).balanceOf(address(this))` to determine how many tokens to send to the swap helper, rather than tracking the exact expected amount from the current operation. This means any residual tokens of the same type as `swapInfo.tokenIn` (which equals the borrow token in `OpenExactIn` flows) will be included in the swap. An attacker can exploit this by monitoring the hook contract for residual token balances, then calling `openPos` or `increasePos` with parameters that use the stuck token as the borrow token. The entire balance—including the stuck tokens—gets swapped to collateral tokens, effectively granting the attacker more collateral than they paid for. This is a well-known anti-pattern: using `balanceOf(this)` instead of precisely tracked amounts allows unintended token absorption.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines in `coreCallback` clearly show the defect. The function reads the full balance and transfers it all to the swap helper without any accounting for the expected amount. The `_increasePosInternal` function sets up the callback with `SwapInfo` where `tokenIn = borrToken`, and the borrow step sends `_borrAmt` to the hook. Any pre-existing balance of `borrToken` in the hook gets swept into the swap. Similarly, `removeCollateral` uses `IERC20(collToken).balanceOf(address(this))` to determine the amount to transfer out, creating a secondary sweep vector.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Uses `balanceOf(address(this))` to determine `amtIn` rather than tracking the expected amount (borrAmt + optional tokenIn). Any residual tokens of the same type as `swapInfo.tokenIn` are included, allowing next user to absorb stuck tokens.

Sets up the callback with `tokenIn = borrToken`. The borrow step sends `_borrAmt` of `borrToken` to the hook. When `coreCallback` runs, it reads the full `borrToken` balance which includes any pre-existing stuck tokens.
Same pattern: uses `balanceOf(address(this))` to determine the output amount. Any stuck collToken in the hook will be sent to the caller.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls increasePos(_posId, borrToken, minimalAmtIn, minimalBorrAmt, data, 0)
2. _increasePosInternal calls _transmitTokenIn(borrToken, minimalAmtIn) -> transfers minimalAmtIn from attacker to hook
3. Multicall step 0: borrow sends minimalBorrAmt of borrToken from pool to hook (address(this))
4. Multicall step 1: callback invokes coreCallback on this hook
5. coreCallback reads amtIn = IERC20(borrToken).balanceOf(address(this)) which = stuckTokens + minimalAmtIn + minimalBorrAmt
6. coreCallback transfers ALL amtIn to swapHelper, which swaps everything to collToken
7. collToken gets deposited as collateral for attacker's position
8. Attacker receives collateral worth stuckTokens + minimalAmtIn + minimalBorrAmt, paying only minimalAmtIn + debt for minimalBorrAmt
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerable path starts at `increasePos` -> `_increasePosInternal` -> multicall(borrow, callback, mintTo, collateralize). In the callback step, `coreCallback` at line ~321 reads `uint amtIn = IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).balanceOf(address(this))` which includes any stuck tokens. At line ~322, it transfers the entire `amtIn` to `swapHelper`. The swap output becomes collateral for the attacker's position, effectively stealing the stuck tokens.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: (1) 1000 USDC are accidentally sent to the MarginTradingHook contract. (2) Attacker monitors for this and calls `openPos` or `increasePos` with `borrPool` being the USDC lending pool. They set `_amtIn = 1` (1 wei of USDC as tokenIn with tokenIn == borrToken) and `_borrAmt = 1`. (3) During `coreCallback`, `balanceOf(address(this))` returns 1000 USDC + 1 wei (from transmitTokenIn) + 1 wei (from borrow) = ~1000 USDC. (4) All 1000 USDC get swapped to collToken and deposited as the attacker's collateral. (5) The attacker's position has ~1000 USDC worth of collateral but only 1 wei of debt. They can withdraw or close the position to extract the value. Net profit: ~1000 USDC minus gas costs.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Any ERC20 tokens that end up in the MarginTradingHook contract—whether from accidental transfers, fee-on-transfer token dust, or other sources—can be stolen by any user who opens or increases a margin position using that token as the borrow token. The severity is HIGH because the loss is 100% of stuck tokens with minimal attacker capital required. The attack is simple, single-block, and requires only standard gas costs.
recommended_mitigations: Track the exact expected token amount rather than using balanceOf. For example, compute `amtIn = _param.amtIn (if tokenIn == borrToken) + _param.borrAmt` and pass it through the SwapInfo struct, then use that value instead of `balanceOf(address(this))`.

Alternatively, snapshot the balance before the multicall and use the delta: `uint balBefore = IERC20(tokenIn).balanceOf(address(this))` at the start, then `uint amtIn = IERC20(tokenIn).balanceOf(address(this)) - balBefore` in the callback. However, this is complex across multicall boundaries.
Add a rescue function (governor-only) to recover accidentally sent tokens, so they don't accumulate as an attack target.
Apply the same fix to `removeCollateral` which also uses `balanceOf(address(this))` for the output amount.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the full system (InitCore, PosManager, LendingPool, MarginTradingHook, Config, Oracle, etc.) on a fork or local testnet. 2. Set up a USDC lending pool and collateral pool (e.g., WETH). 3. Send 1000 USDC directly to the MarginTradingHook contract address (simulating stuck tokens). 4. As the attacker, call `openPos` with minimal `_amtIn` (1 wei USDC) and minimal `_borrAmt` (1 wei), with USDC as borrPool and WETH as collPool. 5. Verify that the position's collateral reflects the full ~1000 USDC value swapped to WETH, not just 2 wei. 6. Close the position and verify the attacker extracts ~1000 USDC worth of value.
uncertainties: The exact amount of stuck tokens that would accumulate in practice under normal operations is unclear—it depends on fee-on-transfer token usage, operational edge cases, and accidental transfers.
The `refundNative` modifier handles WNATIVE cleanup, but whether other ERC20 tokens can be left after normal operations requires deeper testing of all code paths.
additional_testing_needed: Test whether any normal operation flow (openPos, increasePos, reducePos, removeCollateral) can leave ERC20 token dust in the hook under edge conditions.
Test the same pattern in `_reducePosInternal` where `coreCallback` is also used with `CloseExactIn`/`CloseExactOut` swap types.
Test `removeCollateral` which uses the same `balanceOf` pattern for output tokens.
Verify whether the MoeSwapHelper refund mechanism can leave tokens in the hook in case of partial swaps.
